   312→(* -- Collision sub-effect handler ----------------------------------------- *)
   313→(* Handles effects performed by Ox_collide collision functions *)
   314→
   315→let collision_effect_handler (type a) ~is_d2 (eff : a Effect.t)
   316→  : ((a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation -> _) option
   317→  =
   318→  match eff with
   319→  (* New collision effects *)
   320→  | Ox_collide.Add_points_to_score score ->
   321→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   322→      effect_add_points_to_score score;
   323→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   324→  | Ox_collide.Play_collision_sound (sid, seg, px, py, pz) ->
   325→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   326→      effect_play_collision_sound sid seg px py pz;
   327→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   328→  | Ox_collide.Create_awareness_event (objnum, atype) ->
   329→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   330→      effect_create_awareness_event objnum atype;
   331→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   332→  | Ox_collide.Write_back_hit_object packed ->
   333→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   334→      effect_write_back_hit_object packed;
   335→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   336→  | Ox_collide.Apply_damage_to_player (player_objnum, killer_objnum, damage) ->
   337→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   338→      effect_apply_damage_to_player player_objnum killer_objnum damage;
   339→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   340→  (* D1 robot damage effects *)
   341→  | Ox_collide.Increment_kills ->
   342→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   343→      effect_increment_kills ();
   344→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   345→  | Ox_collide.Start_boss_death obj_id ->
   346→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   347→      effect_start_boss_death obj_id;
   348→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   349→  | Ox_collide.Explode_object obj_id ->
   350→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   351→      effect_explode_object obj_id;
   352→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   353→  | Ox_collide.Send_net_robot_explode (obj_id, killer) ->
   354→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   355→      effect_send_net_robot_explode obj_id killer;
   356→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   357→  | Ox_collide.Query_multi_explode (obj_id, killer) ->
   358→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   359→      let r = effect_multi_explode_robot_sub obj_id killer in
   360→      Effect.Deep.continue k r)
   361→  | Ox_collide.Explode_object_delay (obj_id, delay) ->
   362→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   363→      effect_explode_object_delay obj_id delay;
   364→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   365→  (* D2 robot damage effects *)
   366→  | Ox_collide.Set_boss_hit_time ->
   367→    Some (fun (k : (a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation) ->
   368→      effect_set_boss_hit_time ();
   369→      Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   370→  | Ox_collide.Query_player_dead_or_no_shields ->

   546→(* -- Physics sim effect handler ------------------------------------------- *)
   547→
   548→let physics_sim_effect_handler (type a) ~is_d2 (eff : a Effect.t)
   549→  : ((a, _) Effect.Deep.continuation -> _) option
   550→  =
   551→  match eff with
   552→  | Ox_physics_sim.Find_vector_intersection
   553→      (p0x, p0y, p0z, p1x, p1y, p1z, rad, thisobjnum, ignore_list, flags, startseg) ->
   554→    Some
   555→      (fun k ->
   556→        try
   557→        let query =
   558→          [| p0x
   559→           ; p0y
   560→           ; p0z
   561→           ; p1x
   562→           ; p1y
   563→           ; p1z
   564→           ; rad
   565→           ; thisobjnum
   566→           ; flags
   567→           ; startseg
   568→           ; Array.length ignore_list
   569→          |]
   570→        in
   571→        let packed = Array.append query ignore_list in
   572→        let result = effect_find_vector_intersection packed in
   573→        (* Unpack: fate, hit_pnt xyz, hit_seg, hit_side, hit_side_seg,
   574→           hit_object, wallnorm xyz, n_segs, seglist[] *)
   575→        let fate = result.(0) in
   576→        let hit_px = result.(1) in
   577→        let hit_py = result.(2) in
   578→        let hit_pz = result.(3) in
   579→        let hit_seg = result.(4) in
   580→        let hit_side = result.(5) in
   581→        let hit_side_seg = result.(6) in
   582→        let hit_object = result.(7) in
   583→        let wn_x = result.(8) in
   584→        let wn_y = result.(9) in
   585→        let wn_z = result.(10) in
   586→        let n_segs = result.(11) in
   587→        let seglist = Array.sub result ~pos:12 ~len:n_segs in
   588→        Effect.Deep.continue
   589→          k
   590→          ( fate
   591→          , hit_px
   592→          , hit_py
   593→          , hit_pz
   594→          , hit_seg
   595→          , hit_side
   596→          , hit_side_seg
   597→          , hit_object
   598→          , wn_x
   599→          , wn_y
   600→          , wn_z
   601→          , n_segs
   602→          , seglist )
   603→        with Invalid_argument _ ->
   604→          Printf.eprintf "OX_SHADOW: FVI exception, returning HIT_NONE\n";
   605→          Out_channel.flush stderr;
   606→          (* Return HIT_NONE with dummy values *)
   607→          Effect.Deep.continue k (0, p0x, p0y, p0z, startseg, 0, -1, -1, 0, 0, 0, 0, [||]))
   608→  | Ox_physics_sim.Collide_object_with_wall
   609→      (hit_speed, wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz, obj_flags) ->
   610→    Some
   611→      (fun k ->
   612→        let result =
   613→          effect_collide_object_with_wall
   614→            hit_speed
   615→            wall_seg
   616→            wall_side
   617→            hit_px
   618→            hit_py
   619→            hit_pz
   620→            obj_flags
   621→        in
   622→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   623→  | Ox_physics_sim.Scrape_object_on_wall (wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz) ->
   624→    Some
   625→      (fun k ->
   626→        let result =
   627→          effect_scrape_object_on_wall wall_seg wall_side hit_px hit_py hit_pz
   628→        in
   629→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   630→  | Ox_physics_sim.Collide_two_objects (hit_objnum, pos_hit_x, pos_hit_y, pos_hit_z) ->
   631→    Some
   632→      (fun k ->
   633→        (* Fetch collision data from C for both objects *)
   634→        let cd = effect_fetch_collision_data hit_objnum in
   635→        (* Try OCaml dispatch *)
   636→        let dispatch_fn =
   637→          if is_d2 then Ox_collide.collide_two_objects_d2
   638→          else Ox_collide.collide_two_objects_d1
   639→        in
   640→        let ocaml_result =
   641→          Effect.Deep.match_with
   642→            (fun () -> dispatch_fn ~cd ~hit_px:pos_hit_x ~hit_py:pos_hit_y ~hit_pz:pos_hit_z)
   643→            ()
   644→            { retc = (fun x -> x)
   645→            ; exnc = raise
   646→            ; effc = (fun (type b) (eff : b Effect.t) ->
   647→                collision_effect_handler ~is_d2 eff)
   648→            }
   649→        in
   650→        let result =
   651→          match ocaml_result with
   652→          | Some (flags, vx, vy, vz, shields) ->
   653→            effect_write_back_this_object shields flags;
   654→            (flags, vx, vy, vz)
   655→          | None ->
   656→            (* Fall back to C for unported collision types *)
   657→            effect_collide_two_objects hit_objnum pos_hit_x pos_hit_y pos_hit_z
   658→        in
   659→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   660→  | Ox_physics_sim.Obj_relink (objnum, new_seg) ->
   661→    Some
   662→      (fun k ->
   663→        effect_obj_relink objnum new_seg;
   664→        Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   665→  | Ox_physics_sim.Find_object_seg objnum ->
   666→    Some
   667→      (fun k ->
   668→        let result = effect_find_object_seg objnum in
   669→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   670→  | Ox_physics_sim.Update_object_seg objnum ->
   671→    Some
   672→      (fun k ->
   673→        effect_update_object_seg objnum;
   674→        Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   675→  | Ox_physics_sim.Find_point_seg (px, py, pz, seg) ->
   676→    Some
   677→      (fun k ->
   678→        let result = effect_find_point_seg px py pz seg in
   679→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   680→  | Ox_physics_sim.Get_seg_masks (px, py, pz, seg) ->
   681→    Some
   682→      (fun k ->
   683→        let result = effect_get_seg_masks px py pz seg in
   684→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   685→  | Ox_physics_sim.Compute_segment_center seg ->
   686→    Some
   687→      (fun k ->
   688→        let result = effect_compute_segment_center seg in
   689→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   690→  | Ox_physics_sim.Add_stuck_object (wall_seg, wall_side) ->
   691→    Some
   692→      (fun k ->
   693→        effect_add_stuck_object wall_seg wall_side;
   694→        Effect.Deep.continue k ())
   695→  | Ox_physics_sim.Find_connect_side (seg1, seg2) ->
   696→    Some
   697→      (fun k ->
   698→        let result = effect_find_connect_side seg1 seg2 in
   699→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   700→  | Ox_physics_sim.Wall_is_doorway (seg, side) ->
   701→    Some
   702→      (fun k ->
   703→        let result = effect_wall_is_doorway seg side in
   704→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   705→  | Ox_physics_sim.Create_abs_vertex_lists_and_dist (seg, side, spx, spy, spz) ->
   706→    Some
   707→      (fun k ->
   708→        let result = effect_create_abs_vertex_lists_and_dist seg side spx spy spz in
   709→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   710→  | Ox_physics_sim.Tmap_is_force_field (seg, side) ->
   711→    Some
   712→      (fun k ->
   713→        let result = effect_tmap_is_force_field seg side in
   714→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   715→  | Ox_physics_sim.Vm_vector_2_matrix_orient (vx, vy, vz, ux, uy, uz) ->
   716→    Some
   717→      (fun k ->
   718→        let result = effect_vm_vector_2_matrix_orient vx vy vz ux uy uz in
   719→        Effect.Deep.continue k result)
   720→  | Ox_physics_sim.Query_object_type_id objnum ->
   721→    Some
   722→      (fun k ->
   723→        (* Reuse collision data fetch — hit object type at index 20, id at 21 *)
   724→        let cd = effect_fetch_collision_data objnum in
   725→        Effect.Deep.continue k (cd.(20), cd.(21)))
   726→  | _ -> None
   727→;;
   728→
   729→(* D1 bridge function *)
   730→let cd_do_physics_sim_d1
   731→      pos_x
   732→      pos_y
   733→      pos_z
   734→      vel_x
   735→      vel_y
   736→      vel_z
   737→      thrust_x
   738→      thrust_y
   739→      thrust_z
   740→      orient
   741→      rotvel_x
   742→      rotvel_y
   743→      rotvel_z
   744→      rotthrust_x
   745→      rotthrust_y
   746→      rotthrust_z
   747→      size
   748→      mass
   749→      drag
   750→      phys_flags
   751→      obj_flags
   752→      obj_type
   753→      obj_id
   754→      obj_segnum
   755→      objnum
   756→      turnroll
   757→      last_pos_x
   758→      last_pos_y
   759→      last_pos_z
   760→      frame_time
   761→      physics_cheat_flag
   762→  =
   763→  Effect.Deep.match_with
   764→    (fun () ->
   765→       Ox_physics_sim.do_physics_sim_d1
   766→         ~pos_x
   767→         ~pos_y
   768→         ~pos_z
   769→         ~vel_x
   770→         ~vel_y
   771→         ~vel_z
   772→         ~thrust_x
   773→         ~thrust_y
   774→         ~thrust_z
   775→         ~orient
   776→         ~rotvel_x
   777→         ~rotvel_y
   778→         ~rotvel_z
   779→         ~rotthrust_x
   780→         ~rotthrust_y
   781→         ~rotthrust_z
   782→         ~size
   783→         ~mass
   784→         ~drag
   785→         ~phys_flags
   786→         ~obj_flags
   787→         ~obj_type
   788→         ~obj_id
   789→         ~obj_segnum
   790→         ~objnum
   791→         ~turnroll
   792→         ~last_pos_x
   793→         ~last_pos_y
   794→         ~last_pos_z
   795→         ~frame_time
   796→         ~physics_cheat_flag)
   797→    ()
   798→    { retc = (fun x -> x)
   799→    ; exnc = (fun exn ->
   800→        Printf.eprintf "OX_SHADOW: D1 physics_sim exception: %s\n" (Exn.to_string exn);
   801→        Out_channel.flush stderr;
   802→        Array.create ~len:25 0)
   803→    ; effc = (fun (type a) (eff : a Effect.t) -> physics_sim_effect_handler ~is_d2:false eff)
   804→    }
   805→;;
   806→
   807→(* D2 bridge function *)
   808→let cd_do_physics_sim_d2
   809→      pos_x
   810→      pos_y
   811→      pos_z
   812→      vel_x
   813→      vel_y
   814→      vel_z
   815→      thrust_x
   816→      thrust_y
   817→      thrust_z
   818→      orient
   819→      rotvel_x
   820→      rotvel_y
   821→      rotvel_z
   822→      rotthrust_x
   823→      rotthrust_y
   824→      rotthrust_z
   825→      size
   826→      mass
   827→      drag
   828→      phys_flags
   829→      obj_flags
   830→      obj_type
   831→      obj_id
   832→      obj_segnum
   833→      objnum
   834→      turnroll
   835→      last_pos_x
   836→      last_pos_y
   837→      last_pos_z
   838→      orient_uvec_x
   839→      orient_uvec_y
   840→      orient_uvec_z
   841→      seg_special
   842→      frame_time
   843→      physics_cheat_flag
   844→  =
   845→  Effect.Deep.match_with
   846→    (fun () ->
   847→       Ox_physics_sim.do_physics_sim_d2
   848→         ~pos_x
   849→         ~pos_y
   850→         ~pos_z
   851→         ~vel_x
   852→         ~vel_y
   853→         ~vel_z
   854→         ~thrust_x
   855→         ~thrust_y
   856→         ~thrust_z
   857→         ~orient
   858→         ~rotvel_x
   859→         ~rotvel_y
   860→         ~rotvel_z
   861→         ~rotthrust_x
   862→         ~rotthrust_y
   863→         ~rotthrust_z
   864→         ~size
   865→         ~mass
   866→         ~drag
   867→         ~phys_flags
   868→         ~obj_flags
   869→         ~obj_type
   870→         ~obj_id
   871→         ~obj_segnum
   872→         ~objnum
   873→         ~turnroll
   874→         ~last_pos_x
   875→         ~last_pos_y
   876→         ~last_pos_z
   877→         ~orient_uvec_x
   878→         ~orient_uvec_y
   879→         ~orient_uvec_z
   880→         ~seg_special
   881→         ~frame_time
   882→         ~physics_cheat_flag)
   883→    ()
   884→    { retc = (fun x -> x)
   885→    ; exnc = (fun exn ->
   886→        Printf.eprintf "OX_SHADOW: D2 physics_sim exception: %s\n" (Exn.to_string exn);
   887→        Out_channel.flush stderr;
   888→        Array.create ~len:25 0)
   889→    ; effc = (fun (type a) (eff : a Effect.t) -> physics_sim_effect_handler ~is_d2:true eff)
   890→    }
   891→;;
   892→
   893→let () =
   894→  Callback.register "cd_do_physics_sim_d1" cd_do_physics_sim_d1;
   895→  Callback.register "cd_do_physics_sim_d2" cd_do_physics_sim_d2
   896→;;

1→(* Pure game logic for do_physics_sim (D1 + D2).
     2→   FVI retry loop with collision response, using algebraic effects. *)
     3→
     4→let f1_0 = 0x10000
     5→
     6→(* -- Constants ------------------------------------------------------------ *)
     7→
     8→(* Collision fate codes (from fvi.h) *)
     9→let hit_none = 0
    10→let hit_wall = 1
    11→let hit_object = 2
    12→let hit_bad_p0 = 3
    13→
    14→(* FQ flags *)
    15→let fq_check_objs = 1
    16→let fq_transpoint = 4
    17→let fq_get_seglist = 8
    18→
    19→(* Object type codes *)
    20→let obj_player = 4
    21→let obj_weapon = 5
    22→
    23→(* Physics flags *)
    24→let pf_levelling = 0x02
    25→let pf_bounce = 0x04
    26→let pf_stick = 0x10
    27→let pf_persistent = 0x20
    28→let pf_bounced_once = 0x80
    29→let pf_bounces_twice = 0x200
    30→
    31→(* Object flags *)
    32→let of_should_be_dead = 2
    33→
    34→(* Control type *)
    35→let ct_ai = 7
    36→
    37→(* D1: PROXIMITY_ID = 16 *)
    38→let proximity_id = 16
    39→
    40→(* D2: SUPERPROX_ID = 38 *)
    41→let superprox_id = 38
    42→
    43→(* D2: MAX_OBJECT_VEL = i2f(100) *)
    44→let max_object_vel = 100 * f1_0
    45→
    46→(* WID_FLY_FLAG *)
    47→let wid_fly_flag = 1
    48→
    49→(* Max retries *)
    50→let max_fvi_segs = 100
    51→let max_ignore_objs = 100
    52→
    53→(* D2: SEGMENT_IS_CONTROLCEN *)
    54→let segment_is_controlcen = 1
    55→
    56→(* -- Algebraic effects ---------------------------------------------------- *)
    57→
    58→type _ Effect.t +=
    59→  | Find_vector_intersection :
    60→      (int
    61→      * int
    62→      * int (* p0 *)
    63→      * int
    64→      * int
    65→      * int (* p1 *)
    66→      * int (* rad *)
    67→      * int (* thisobjnum *)
    68→      * int array (* ignore_obj_list, terminated by -1 *)
    69→      * int (* flags *)
    70→      * int (* startseg *))
    71→      -> (int (* fate *)
    72→         * int
    73→         * int
    74→         * int (* hit_pnt *)
    75→         * int (* hit_seg *)
    76→         * int (* hit_side *)
    77→         * int (* hit_side_seg *)
    78→         * int (* hit_object *)
    79→         * int
    80→         * int
    81→         * int (* hit_wallnorm *)
    82→         * int (* n_segs *)
    83→         * int array (* seglist *))
    84→           Effect.t
    85→  | Collide_object_with_wall :
    86→      (int * int * int * int * int * int * int)
    87→      -> (* hit_speed, wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz, obj_flags_out *)
    88→      int Effect.t (* returns updated obj_flags *)
    89→  | Scrape_object_on_wall :
    90→      (int * int * int * int * int)
    91→      -> (* wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz *)
    92→      int Effect.t (* returns updated obj_flags *)
    93→  | Collide_two_objects :
    94→      (int * int * int * int)
    95→      -> (* hit_objnum, pos_hit_x, pos_hit_y, pos_hit_z *)
    96→      (int (* obj_flags_out *) * int * int * int (* new velocity *)) Effect.t
    97→  | Obj_relink : (int * int) -> unit Effect.t (* objnum, new_seg *)
    98→  | Find_object_seg : int -> int Effect.t (* objnum -> seg or -1 *)
    99→  | Update_object_seg : int -> unit Effect.t (* objnum *)
   100→  | Find_point_seg :
   101→      (int * int * int * int)
   102→      -> int Effect.t (* px, py, pz, seg -> seg or -1 *)
   103→  | Get_seg_masks :
   104→      (int * int * int * int)
   105→      -> int Effect.t (* px, py, pz, seg -> centermask *)
   106→  | Compute_segment_center : int -> (int * int * int) Effect.t (* seg -> cx, cy, cz *)
   107→  | Add_stuck_object : (int * int) -> unit Effect.t (* wall_seg, wall_side *)
   108→  | Find_connect_side : (int * int) -> int Effect.t (* seg1, seg2 -> sidenum or -1 *)
   109→  | Wall_is_doorway : (int * int) -> int Effect.t (* seg, side -> doorway flags *)
   110→  | Create_abs_vertex_lists_and_dist :
   111→      (int * int * int * int * int)
   112→      -> (* seg, side, start_px, start_py, start_pz *)
   113→      (int * int * int * int) Effect.t (* dist, normal_x, normal_y, normal_z *)
   114→  | (* D2 only: check if a wall segment tmap has forcefield flag *)
   115→      Tmap_is_force_field :
   116→      (int * int)
   117→      -> int Effect.t (* seg, side -> 0 or 1 *)
   118→  | (* Query object type and id by objnum *)
   119→      Query_object_type_id : int -> (int * int) Effect.t (* objnum -> (type, id) *)
   120→  | (* D2 only: vm_vector_2_matrix for weapon orientation on bounce *)
   121→      Vm_vector_2_matrix_orient :
   122→      (int * int * int * int * int * int)
   123→      -> (* vel xyz, orient_uvec xyz *)
   124→      (int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int) Effect.t (* orient 9 *)
   125→
   126→(* -- Helper: build ignore list array -------------------------------------- *)
   127→
   128→let make_ignore_list objs n =
   129→  let arr = Array.create ~len:(n + 1) (-1) in
   130→  for i = 0 to n - 1 do
   131→    arr.(i) <- objs.(i)
   132→  done;
   133→  arr
   134→;;
   135→
   136→(* -- D1: do_physics_sim --------------------------------------------------- *)
   137→
   138→let do_physics_sim_d1
   139→      (* Object state *)
   140→      ~pos_x
   141→      ~pos_y
   142→      ~pos_z
   143→      ~vel_x
   144→      ~vel_y
   145→      ~vel_z
   146→      ~thrust_x
   147→      ~thrust_y
   148→      ~thrust_z
   149→      ~orient (* 9-element: rvec xyz, uvec xyz, fvec xyz *)
   150→      ~rotvel_x
   151→      ~rotvel_y
   152→      ~rotvel_z
   153→      ~rotthrust_x
   154→      ~rotthrust_y
   155→      ~rotthrust_z
   156→      ~size
   157→      ~mass
   158→      ~drag
   159→      ~phys_flags
   160→      ~obj_flags
   161→      ~obj_type
   162→      ~obj_id:_obj_id
   163→      ~obj_segnum
   164→      ~objnum
   165→      ~turnroll
   166→      ~last_pos_x
   167→      ~last_pos_y
   168→      ~last_pos_z
   169→      (* Game state *)
   170→      ~frame_time
   171→      ~physics_cheat_flag
   172→  =
   173→  (* do_physics_sim_rot *)
   174→  let rvx, rvy, rvz, orient_r, turnroll_out =
   175→    match
   176→      Ox_physics.do_physics_sim_rot
   177→        ~rotvel:(rotvel_x, rotvel_y, rotvel_z)
   178→        ~rotthrust:(rotthrust_x, rotthrust_y, rotthrust_z)
   179→        ~orient
   180→        ~drag
   181→        ~mass
   182→        ~flags:phys_flags
   183→        ~turnroll
   184→        ~frame_time
   185→    with
   186→    | Some (orient_out, rotvel_out, turnroll_out) ->
   187→      let rvx, rvy, rvz = rotvel_out in
   188→      rvx, rvy, rvz, orient_out, turnroll_out
   189→    | None -> rotvel_x, rotvel_y, rotvel_z, orient, turnroll
   190→  in
   191→  (* Early exit if no velocity/thrust *)
   192→  let (o_rx, o_ry, o_rz), (o_ux, o_uy, o_uz), (o_fx, o_fy, o_fz) = orient_r in
   193→  if
   194→    not
   195→      (vel_x <> 0
   196→       || vel_y <> 0
   197→       || vel_z <> 0
   198→       || thrust_x <> 0
   199→       || thrust_y <> 0
   200→       || thrust_z <> 0)
   201→  then
   202→    [| pos_x
   203→     ; pos_y
   204→     ; pos_z
   205→     ; vel_x
   206→     ; vel_y
   207→     ; vel_z
   208→     ; o_rx
   209→     ; o_ry
   210→     ; o_rz
   211→     ; o_ux
   212→     ; o_uy
   213→     ; o_uz
   214→     ; o_fx
   215→     ; o_fy
   216→     ; o_fz
   217→     ; obj_segnum
   218→     ; obj_flags
   219→     ; phys_flags
   220→     ; turnroll_out
   221→     ; rvx
   222→     ; rvy
   223→     ; rvz
   224→     ; 0 (* retry_count *)
   225→     ; 0 (* n_phys_segs *)
   226→     ; 0 (* needs_levelling *)
   227→    |]
   228→  else (
   229→    let sim_time = ref frame_time in
   230→    (* Drag *)
   231→    let vel_x, vel_y, vel_z =
   232→      if drag <> 0
   233→      then (
   234→        let packed =
   235→          [| vel_x
   236→           ; vel_y
   237→           ; vel_z
   238→           ; thrust_x
   239→           ; thrust_y
   240→           ; thrust_z
   241→           ; drag
   242→           ; mass
   243→           ; phys_flags
   244→           ; !sim_time
   245→          |]
   246→        in
   247→        let result = Ox_physics.do_physics_drag packed in
   248→        result.(0), result.(1), result.(2))
   249→      else vel_x, vel_y, vel_z
   250→    in
   251→    let cur_pos_x = ref pos_x in
   252→    let cur_pos_y = ref pos_y in
   253→    let cur_pos_z = ref pos_z in
   254→    let cur_vel_x = ref vel_x in
   255→    let cur_vel_y = ref vel_y in
   256→    let cur_vel_z = ref vel_z in
   257→    let cur_segnum = ref obj_segnum in
   258→    let cur_flags = ref obj_flags in
   259→    let cur_phys_flags = ref phys_flags in
   260→    let start_pos_x = pos_x in
   261→    let start_pos_y = pos_y in
   262→    let start_pos_z = pos_z in
   263→    let orig_segnum = obj_segnum in
   264→    let obj_stopped = ref false in
   265→    let retry_count = ref 0 in
   266→    let n_ignore_objs = ref 0 in
   267→    let ignore_objs = Array.create ~len:max_ignore_objs (-1) in
   268→    let n_phys_segs = ref 0 in
   269→    let phys_seglist = Array.create ~len:max_fvi_segs 0 in
   270→    let try_again = ref true in
   271→    let early_return = ref false in
   272→    ignore phys_seglist;
   273→    (* seglist is tracked but returned via n_phys_segs count *)
   274→    while !try_again && not !early_return do
   275→      try_again := false;
   276→      (* frame_vec = velocity * sim_time *)
   277→      let fvx, fvy, fvz =
   278→        Ox_math.vm_vec_copy_scale ~v:(!cur_vel_x, !cur_vel_y, !cur_vel_z) ~k:!sim_time
   279→      in
   280→      if fvx = 0 && fvy = 0 && fvz = 0
   281→      then () (* break *)
   282→      else (
   283→        retry_count := !retry_count + 1;
   284→        if !retry_count > 3 && not (obj_type = obj_player && !retry_count <= 8)
   285→        then () (* break: too many retries *)
   286→        else (
   287→        let new_px, new_py, new_pz =
   288→          Ox_math.vm_vec_add ~a:(!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z) ~b:(fvx, fvy, fvz)
   289→        in
   290→        let ignore_list = make_ignore_list ignore_objs !n_ignore_objs in
   291→        let fq_flags = ref fq_check_objs in
   292→        if obj_type = obj_weapon then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_transpoint;
   293→        if obj_type = obj_player then fq_flags := !fq_flags lor fq_get_seglist;
   294→        let ( fate
   295→            , hit_px
   296→            , hit_py
   297→            , hit_pz
   298→            , hit_seg
   299→            , hit_side
   300→            , hit_side_seg
   301→            , hit_objnum
   302→            , wn_x
   303→            , wn_y
   304→            , wn_z
   305→            , n_segs
   306→            , seglist )
   307→          =
   308→          Effect.perform
   309→            (Find_vector_intersection
   310→               ( !cur_pos_x
   311→               , !cur_pos_y
   312→               , !cur_pos_z
   313→               , new_px
   314→               , new_py
   315→               , new_pz
   316→               , size
   317→               , objnum
   318→               , ignore_list
   319→               , !fq_flags
   320→               , !cur_segnum ))
   321→        in
   322→        (* D1: proximity mine hack — don't count hitting a proximity mine as a retry *)
   323→        if fate = hit_object && hit_objnum >= 0
   324→        then (
   325→          let (ho_type, ho_id) = Effect.perform (Query_object_type_id hit_objnum) in
   326→          if ho_type = obj_weapon && ho_id = proximity_id
   327→          then retry_count := !retry_count - 1);
   328→        (* Player seglist tracking *)
   329→        if obj_type = obj_player
   330→        then (
   331→          if
   332→            !n_phys_segs > 0
   333→            && n_segs > 0
   334→            && phys_seglist.(!n_phys_segs - 1) = seglist.(0)
   335→          then n_phys_segs := !n_phys_segs - 1;
   336→          let i = ref 0 in
   337→          while !i < n_segs && !n_phys_segs < max_fvi_segs - 1 do
   338→            phys_seglist.(!n_phys_segs) <- seglist.(!i);
   339→            n_phys_segs := !n_phys_segs + 1;
   340→            i := !i + 1
   341→          done);
   342→        if hit_seg = -1
   343→        then (
   344→          (* Horrible error — kill weapons, break *)
   345→          if obj_type = obj_weapon then cur_flags := !cur_flags lor of_should_be_dead;
   346→          (* break *)
   347→          ())
   348→        else (
   349→          let save_pos_x = !cur_pos_x in
   350→          let save_pos_y = !cur_pos_y in
   351→          let save_pos_z = !cur_pos_z in
   352→          let save_seg = !cur_segnum in
   353→          cur_pos_x := hit_px;
   354→          cur_pos_y := hit_py;
   355→          cur_pos_z := hit_pz;
   356→          if hit_seg <> !cur_segnum
   357→          then (
   358→            Effect.perform (Obj_relink (objnum, hit_seg));
   359→            cur_segnum := hit_seg);
   360→          (* Check if position is valid *)
   361→          let centermask =
   362→            Effect.perform
   363→              (Get_seg_masks (!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z, !cur_segnum))
   364→          in
   365→          if centermask <> 0
   366→          then (
   367→            let n = Effect.perform (Find_object_seg objnum) in
   368→            if n = -1
   369→            then (
   370→              if obj_type = obj_player
   371→              then (
   372→                let n2 =
   373→                  Effect.perform
   374→                    (Find_point_seg (last_pos_x, last_pos_y, last_pos_z, !cur_segnum))
   375→                in
   376→                if n2 <> -1
   377→                then (
   378→                  cur_pos_x := last_pos_x;
   379→                  cur_pos_y := last_pos_y;
   380→                  cur_pos_z := last_pos_z;
   381→                  Effect.perform (Obj_relink (objnum, n2));
   382→                  cur_segnum := n2)
   383→                else (
   384→                  let cx, cy, cz = Effect.perform (Compute_segment_center !cur_segnum) in
   385→                  cur_pos_x := cx + objnum;
   386→                  cur_pos_y := cy;
   387→                  cur_pos_z := cz))
   388→              else (
   389→                let cx, cy, cz = Effect.perform (Compute_segment_center !cur_segnum) in
   390→                cur_pos_x := cx + objnum;
   391→                cur_pos_y := cy;
   392→                cur_pos_z := cz);
   393→              if obj_type = obj_weapon then cur_flags := !cur_flags lor of_should_be_dead)
   394→            else ();
   395→            (* C does unconditional return when centermask != 0 *)
   396→            early_return := true);
   397→          if not !early_return
   398→          then (
   399→            (* Calculate new sim_time *)
   400→            let moved_time = ref 0 in
   401→            let _actual_dist, (mn_x, mn_y, mn_z) =
   402→              Ox_math.vm_vec_normalized_dir
   403→                ~v_end:(!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z)
   404→                ~v_start:(save_pos_x, save_pos_y, save_pos_z)
   405→            in
   406→            let actual_dist = _actual_dist in
   407→            if
   408→              fate = hit_wall
   409→              && Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(mn_x, mn_y, mn_z) ~b:(fvx, fvy, fvz) < 0
   410→            then (
   411→              (* Moved backwards — restore position *)
   412→              cur_pos_x := save_pos_x;
   413→              cur_pos_y := save_pos_y;
   414→              cur_pos_z := save_pos_z;
   415→              Effect.perform (Obj_relink (objnum, save_seg));
   416→              cur_segnum := save_seg;
   417→              moved_time := 0)
   418→            else (
   419→              let attempted_dist = Ox_math.vm_vec_mag ~v:(fvx, fvy, fvz) in
   420→              let old_sim_time = !sim_time in
   421→              sim_time
   422→              := Ox_math.fixmuldiv
   423→                   ~a:!sim_time
   424→                   ~b:(attempted_dist - actual_dist)
   425→                   ~c:attempted_dist;
   426→              moved_time := old_sim_time - !sim_time;
   427→              if !sim_time < 0 || !sim_time > old_sim_time
   428→              then (
   429→                sim_time := old_sim_time;
   430→                moved_time := 0));
   431→            (* Collision response *)
   432→            match fate with
   433→            | f when f = hit_wall ->
   434→              let mv_x, mv_y, mv_z =
   435→                Ox_math.vm_vec_sub
   436→                  ~a:(!cur_pos_x, !cur_pos_y, !cur_pos_z)
   437→                  ~b:(save_pos_x, save_pos_y, save_pos_z)
   438→              in
   439→              let wall_part =
   440→                Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod ~a:(mv_x, mv_y, mv_z) ~b:(wn_x, wn_y, wn_z)
   441→              in
   442→              if
   443→                wall_part <> 0
   444→                && !moved_time > 0
   445→                && -Ox_math.fixdiv ~a:wall_part ~b:!moved_time > 0
   446→              then (
   447→                let hit_speed = -Ox_math.fixdiv ~a:wall_part ~b:!moved_time in
   448→                let new_flags =
   449→                  Effect.perform
   450→                    (Collide_object_with_wall
   451→                       ( hit_speed
   452→                       , hit_side_seg
   453→                       , hit_side
   454→                       , hit_px
   455→                       , hit_py
   456→                       , hit_pz
   457→                       , !cur_flags ))
   458→                in
   459→                cur_flags := new_flags)
   460→              else (
   461→                let new_flags =
   462→                  Effect.perform
   463→                    (Scrape_object_on_wall (hit_side_seg, hit_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz))
   464→                in
   465→                cur_flags := new_flags);
   466→              if not (!cur_flags land of_should_be_dead <> 0)
   467→              then
   468→                if !cur_phys_flags land pf_stick <> 0
   469→                then (
   470→                  (* Stick to wall *)
   471→                  Effect.perform (Add_stuck_object (hit_side_seg, hit_side));
   472→                  cur_vel_x := 0;
   473→                  cur_vel_y := 0;
   474→                  cur_vel_z := 0;
   475→                  obj_stopped := true;
   476→                  try_again := false)
   477→                else (
   478→                  (* Slide along wall *)
   479→                  let wall_part2 =
   480→                    Ox_math.vm_vec_dotprod
   481→                      ~a:(wn_x, wn_y, wn_z)
   482→                      ~b:(!cur_vel_x, !cur_vel_y, !cur_vel_z)
   483→                  in
   484→                  let wall_part2 =
   485→                    if !cur_phys_flags land pf_bounce <> 0
   486→                    then wall_part2 * 2
   487→                    else wall_part2
   488→                  in
   489→                  let nvx, nvy, nvz =
   490→                    Ox_math.vm_vec_scale_add2
   491→                      ~dest:(!cur_vel_x, !cur_vel_y, !cur_vel_z)
   492→                      ~src:(wn_x, wn_y, wn_z)
   493→                      ~k:(-wall_part2)
   494→                  in
   495→                  cur_vel_x := nvx;
   496→                  cur_vel_y := nvy;
   497→                  cur_vel_z := nvz;
   498→                  try_again := true)
   499→            | f when f = hit_object ->
   500→              (* Calculate hit point between two objects *)

input.description: Find functions that apply rotation or bump objects

content.text:
Excellent! Line 1773 shows `physics_turn_towards_vector(force_vec, obj, rate);` which modifies the object's orientation. This is called by `phys_apply_rot`, which is called by collision handlers like `collide_player_and_wall` and `bump_this_object`.

Now I have a complete picture. Let me create a summary document with my findings.

## Summary

Based on my analysis of the ChocolateDescent codebase, I've identified the root cause of the position and orientation divergences in object 92 starting at frame 43:

### The Core Problem

**The unported C function most likely responsible is `phys_apply_rot()` in `main_d2/physics.cpp` (line 1735).**

Here's the call chain:

1. **Collision Response** → `collide_object_with_wall()` or `collide_player_and_wall()` in `main_d2/collide.cpp`
2. → **Calls** `bump_this_object()` or `phys_apply_rot()` directly
3. → **Calls** `physics_turn_towards_vector()` (line 1773 in physics.cpp)
4. → **Modifies** object's `orient` (orientation matrix)

### Why the Divergence Occurs

In `ox/physics_sim_bridge.ml` (lines 449-465), when OCaml encounters a wall collision, it performs:

```ocaml
| f when f = hit_wall ->
  let new_flags = Effect.perform (Collide_object_with_wall (...))
```

The effect handler in `main_d2/physics.cpp` (lines 504-515) calls the C function:

```cpp
ps_obj->pos = hp;
collide_object_with_wall(ps_obj, (fix)hit_speed, wall_seg, wall_side, &hp);
return ps_obj->flags;  // <- Only returns flags!
```

**The problem:** `collide_object_with_wall()` can call `phys_apply_rot()` which modifies `ps_obj->orient`, but the effect handler **only returns the updated `flags`**, not the updated orientation.

### Evidence

1. **Bytes 32-33 are orient.rvec components** (from CLAUDE.md: "32 orient.rvec.x 36 orient.rvec.y 40 orient.rvec.z")

2. **`phys_apply_rot()` modifies orientation:**
   - Called by `collide_player_and_wall()` at line 345 for forcefield hits
   - Called by `bump_this_object()` at line 265 when robots collide
   - Calls `physics_turn_towards_vector()` which rotates the object matrix

3. **OCaml never reads back the orientation changes:**
   - In `ox_physics_sim.ml`, the orientation (`orient_r`) is only set at the start via `do_physics_sim_rot()` (lines 174-190 for D1, 711-726 for D2)
   - After collision effects, the orientation is never updated
   - The final return (lines 635-665) uses the stale `o_rx, o_ry, o_rz` values

### Why Position Diverges

**`collide_object_with_wall()` and `scrape_object_on_wall()` can also call `bump_one_object()` or `bump_two_objects()`, which call `phys_apply_force()` to modify velocity.** This force application can cause the object to end up in a different segment, which then causes `find_object_seg()` or `update_object_seg()` to trigger during the next physics frame, resulting in position changes that OCaml's current position tracking doesn't account for.

Additionally:
- Line 512 in physics.cpp syncs `ps_obj->pos = hp` before collision handlers, but this position update in the C object isn't reflected back to OCaml
- Collision handlers may call `add_stuck_object()`, `add_points_to_score()`, and other effects that affect subsequent physics calculations

### Functions That Need Porting

To fix this divergence, you need to port to OCaml:

1. **`phys_apply_rot()`** - applies rotational force to modify object orientation
2. **`physics_turn_towards_vector()`** - the actual rotation computation
3. **`phys_apply_force()`** - applies linear force (velocity changes)
4. **Collision-specific rotations** like those in `collide_player_and_wall()` and `collide_weapon_and_wall()`

The divergence tells you exactly what's missing: position and orientation physics calculations that happen during wall collisions are implemented in C but not ported to OCaml yet.

file.content:
~obj_flags
      ~obj_type
      ~obj_id:_obj_id
      ~obj_segnum
      ~objnum
      ~turnroll
      ~last_pos_x
      ~last_pos_y
      ~last_pos_z
      (* Game state *)
      ~frame_time
      ~physics_cheat_flag
  =
  (* do_physics_sim_rot *)
  let rvx, rvy, rvz, orient_r, turnroll_out =
    match
      Ox_physics.do_physics_sim_rot
        ~rotvel:(rotvel_x, rotvel_y, rotvel_z)
        ~rotthrust:(rotthrust_x, rotthrust_y, rotthrust_z)
        ~orient
        ~drag
        ~mass
        ~flags:phys_flags
        ~turnroll
        ~frame_time
    with
    | Some (orient_out, rotvel_out, turnroll_out) ->
      let rvx, rvy, rvz = rotvel_out in
      rvx, rvy, rvz, orient_out, turnroll_out
    | None -> rotvel_x, rotvel_y, rotvel_z, orient, turnroll
  in
  (* Early exit if no velocity/thrust *)
  let (o_rx, o_ry, o_rz), (o_ux, o_uy, o_uz), (o_fx, o_fy, o_fz) = orient_r in
  if
    not
      (vel_x <> 0
       || vel_y <> 0
       || vel_z <> 0
       || thrust_x <> 0
       || thrust_y <> 0
       || thrust_z <> 0)
  then
    [| pos_x
     ; pos_y
     ; pos_z

content.thinking:
So the orientation is computed once at the start via `do_physics_sim_rot`, and then later when wall collisions happen, the C `collide_object_with_wall` effect might modify the orientation, but only returns flags. The OCaml code uses the stale orientation.

But wait - per the project rules, the fix is NOT to sync state back. The fix is to port the collision functions to OCaml. But which collision function is causing this specific divergence?

Actually, let me think about this differently. Object 92 - what type is it? The divergence starts at frame 43 with position and orientation bytes. Let me check if there's a simpler explanation.


The orientation diverges significantly at bytes 32-33 in frame 43 (A=0x0121 vs B=0x003B), and positions shift substantially too. If orientations are diverging this early, the physics simulation would compound the error each subsequent frame. Something's changing object 92's state around frame 43 - either the physics sim itself or something from AI or collision detection.

Let me trace through `do_physics_sim_rot` in ox_physics.ml and see if there's rotation or force application happening that OCaml isn't handling. I need to check where `phys_apply_rot` gets called in the C code - the agent mentioned it's invoked by collision handlers - and whether that's actually ported over to OCaml.

content.text: Let me check if `phys_apply_rot` and `phys_apply_force` are ported to OCaml.

content.thinking:
So `phys_apply_force` and `phys_apply_rot` are already ported to OCaml AND called from the C bridge. When C collision code calls `phys_apply_force`, it routes through the OCaml implementation via bridge.

So the issue isn't that these functions are unported. The issue is that when `collide_object_with_wall` or `scrape_object_on_wall` is called as a C effect from the OCaml physics sim, those C functions modify the object's velocity, orientation etc. in C, but the OCaml physics sim only gets back `flags` and doesn't see those changes.


The fix is to port the collision handlers to OCaml so those state modifications happen there instead. But I need to identify what object 92 actually is and why it's diverging in position and orientation—the collision handler being called for it must be the culprit, since the effect only returns flags while the C version modifies state directly. an effect, C modifies the C-side object, but OCaml keeps its own state. Then OCaml writes back its stale state at the end, overwriting the C modifications.

This is the classic divergence pattern described in CLAUDE.md. The fix is to port collide_object_with_wall to OCaml.

But `collide_object_with_wall` dispatches to specific handlers based on object type (player+wall, robot+wall, weapon+wall). Let me figure out what type object 92 is to know which handler to port.

Actually, I can't easily determine the object type from the logs. But looking at the divergence - it starts at frame 43 with just position and orientation bytes. Given that `phys_apply_force` and `phys_apply_rot` are already ported, the issue is specifically in the wall collision handler.

I need to look at the C implementation of `collide_object_with_wall` to see what it's doing beyond the physics calls I've already ported.

content.thinking:
Now I see the issue clearly. When `collide_object_with_wall` is called as a C effect:

1. C receives the hit point and updates `ps_obj->pos = hp`
2. C calls `collide_object_with_wall(ps_obj, ...)` which may:
   - Call `bump_one_object` or similar, which calls `phys_apply_force` (modifying velocity)
   - Call `phys_apply_rot` (modifying rotational velocity)
   - These go through OCaml bridge, BUT the OCaml physics sim doesn't see the results
3. 

Only `ps_obj->flags` gets returned to OCaml. The real problem is that `phys_apply_force` and `phys_apply_rot` route through OCaml via the bridge, but they modify the C object's velocity and rotational velocity directly—not the OCaml physics sim's local state. So OCaml keeps running with stale values. Let me check the bridge implementation to see if it actually writes back to the C object or if there's a disconnect there. I see the issue now—when `collide_object_with_wall` calls `phys_apply_force` through the OCaml bridge during collision handling, it computes and writes the new velocity back to the C object, but OCaml's physics simulation never sees that update since it maintains its own separate velocity state. The fix is to port the wall collision dispatcher itself to OCaml so the velocity changes happen within the same simulation context. Let me trace through what `collide_object_with_wall` actually does in C to understand which handlers need porting.

   FVI retry loop with collision response, using algebraic effects. *)

let f1_0 = 0x10000

(* -- Constants ------------------------------------------------------------ *)

(* Collision fate codes (from fvi.h) *)
let hit_none = 0
let hit_wall = 1
let hit_object = 2
let hit_bad_p0 = 3

(* FQ flags *)
let fq_check_objs = 1
let fq_transpoint = 4

(* Object type codes *)
let obj_player = 4
let obj_weapon = 5

(* Physics flags *)

let pf_stick = 0x10
let pf_persistent = 0x20
let pf_bounced_once = 0x80
let pf_bounces_twice = 0x200

(* D2: SUPERPROX_ID = 38 *)

(* D2: MAX_OBJECT_VEL = i2f(100) *)
let max_object_vel = 100 * f1_0

(* WID_FLY_FLAG *)
let wid_fly_flag = 1

(* Max retries *)
let max_fvi_segs = 100

(* -- Algebraic effects ---------------------------------------------------- *)

type _ Effect.t +=
  | Find_vector_intersection :
      (int
      * int
      * int (* p0 *)
      * int
      * int
      * int (* p1 *)
      * int (* rad *)
      * int (* thisobjnum *)
      * int array (* ignore_obj_list, terminated by -1 *)
      * int (* flags *)
      * int (* startseg *))
      -> (int (* fate *)
         * int
         * int
         * int (* hit_pnt *)
         * int (* hit_seg *)
         * int (* hit_side *)
         * int (* hit_side_seg *)
         * int (* hit_object *)
         * int
         * int
         * int (* hit_wallnorm *)
         * int (* n_segs *)
         * int array (* seglist *))
           Effect.t
  | Collide_object_with_wall :
      (int * int * int * int * int * int * int)
      -> (* hit_speed, wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz, obj_flags_out *)
      int Effect.t (* returns updated obj_flags *)
  | Scrape_object_on_wall :
      (int * int * int * int * int)
      -> (* wall_seg, wall_side, hit_px, hit_py, hit_pz *)
      int Effect.t (* returns updated obj_flags *)
  | Collide_two_objects :
      (int * int * int * int)
      -> (* hit_objnum, pos_hit_x, pos_hit_y, pos_hit_z *)
      (int (* obj_flags_out *) * int * int * int (* new velocity *)) Effect.t
  | Obj_relink : (int * int) -> unit Effect.t (* objnum, new_seg *)
  | Find_object_seg : int -> int Effect.t (* objnum -> seg or -1 *)
  | Update_object_seg : int -> unit Effect.t (* objnum *)
  | Find_point_seg :
      (int * int * int * int)
      -> int Effect.t (* px, py, pz, seg -> seg or -1 *)
  | Get_seg_masks :
      (int * int * int * int)
      -> int Effect.t (* px, py, pz, seg -> centermask *)
  | Compute_segment_center : int -> (int * int * int) Effect.t (* seg -> cx, cy, cz *)
  | Add_stuck_object : (int * int) -> unit Effect.t (* wall_seg, wall_side *)
  | Find_connect_side : (int * int) -> int Effect.t (* seg1, seg2 -> sidenum or -1 *)
  | Wall_is_doorway : (int * int) -> int Effect.t (* seg, side -> doorway flags *)
  | Create_abs_vertex_lists_and_dist :
      (int * int * int * int * int)
      -> (* seg, side, start_px, start_py, start_pz *)
      (int * int * int * int) Effect.t (* dist, normal_x, normal_y, normal_z *)
  | (* D2 only: check if a wall segment tmap has forcefield flag *)
      Tmap_is_force_field :
      (int * int)
      -> int Effect.t (* seg, side -> 0 or 1 *)
  | (* Query object type and id by objnum *)
      Query_object_type_id : int -> (int * int) Effect.t (* objnum -> (type, id) *)
  | (* D2 only: vm_vector_2_matrix for weapon orientation on bounce *)
      Vm_vector_2_matrix_orient :
      (int * int * int * int * int * int)
      -> (* vel xyz, orient_uvec xyz *)
      (int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int * int) Effect.t (* orient 9 *)

(* -- Helper: build ignore list array -------------------------------------- *)

let make_ignore_list objs n =
  let arr = Array.create ~len:(n + 1) (-1) in
  for i = 0 to n - 1 do
    arr.(i) <- objs.(i)
  done;
  arr
;;

      ~obj_segnum
      ~objnum
      ~turnroll
      ~last_pos_x
      ~last_pos_y
      ~last_pos_z
      (* Game state *)

file.content:
(* Pure physics helper functions ported from main_d1/physics.cpp *)

let f1_0 = 0x10000

(* Saturating rotational velocity assignment.
   If delta and dest have opposite signs and delta is small,
   damp by dividing delta by 4. Otherwise just assign delta.
   C original: physics.cpp physics_set_rotvel_and_saturate *)
let physics_set_rotvel_and_saturate ~dest ~delta =
  if delta lxor dest < 0
  then if Int.abs delta < f1_0 / 8 then delta / 4 else delta
  else delta
;;

(* Compute new rotational velocity to steer towards goal_vector.
   C original: physics.cpp physics_turn_towards_vector

   Inputs:
     goal       - target direction vector (x, y, z)
     fvec       - object's current forward vector (x, y, z)
     rate       - turning rate (higher = slower turn)
     is_morph   - if true, double the rate (morph objects turn slower)
     cur_rotvel - current rotational velocity (rx, ry, rz)

   Returns: new rotational velocity (rx, ry, rz).
   If goal is the zero vector, returns cur_rotvel unchanged. *)
let physics_turn_towards_vector ~goal ~fvec ~rate ~is_morph ~cur_rotvel =
  let gx, gy, gz = goal in
  if gx = 0 && gy = 0 && gz = 0
  then cur_rotvel

file.content:
let do_physics_sim_rot ~rotvel ~rotthrust ~orient ~drag ~mass ~flags ~turnroll ~frame_time
  =
  let rvx, rvy, rvz = rotvel in
  let rtx, rty, rtz = rotthrust in
  (* Early exit if nothing to do *)
  if rvx = 0 && rvy = 0 && rvz = 0 && rtx = 0 && rty = 0 && rtz = 0
  then None
  else (
    (* Apply drag to rotational velocity *)
    let rotvel =
      if drag <> 0
      then (
        let count = frame_time / ft in
        let r = frame_time mod ft in
        let k = Ox_math.fixdiv ~a:r ~b:ft in
        let drag_scaled = drag * 5 / 2 in
        if flags land pf_uses_thrust <> 0
        then (
          let accel = vec_scale ~v:rotthrust ~s:(Ox_math.fixdiv ~a:f1_0 ~b:mass) in
          (* Iterate count times: rotvel += accel; rotvel *= (1 - drag) *)
          let rv = ref rotvel in
          for _ = 1 to count do
            rv := vec_scale ~v:(vec_add ~a:!rv ~b:accel) ~s:(f1_0 - drag_scaled)
          done;
          (* Linear scale on remaining bit *)
          let rv = vec_scale_add2 ~v:!rv ~other:accel ~s:k in
          vec_scale ~v:rv ~s:(f1_0 - Ox_math.fixmul ~a:k ~b:drag_scaled))
        else if flags land pf_free_spinning = 0
        then (
          (* No thrust, not free spinning: just apply drag *)
          let total_drag = ref f1_0 in
          for _ = 1 to count do
            total_drag := Ox_math.fixmul ~a:!total_drag ~b:(f1_0 - drag_scaled)
          done;
          let total_drag =
            Ox_math.fixmul ~a:!total_drag ~b:(f1_0 - Ox_math.fixmul ~a:k ~b:drag_scaled)
          in
          vec_scale ~v:rotvel ~s:total_drag)
        else
          (* Free spinning: no drag applied *)
          rotvel)
      else rotvel
    in
    (* Unrotate for bank caused by turnroll *)
    let orient =
      if turnroll <> 0
      then (
        let rotmat = Ox_math.vm_angles_2_matrix ~v:(0, -turnroll, 0) in
        Ox_math.vm_matrix_x_matrix ~a:orient ~b:rotmat)
      else orient
    in
    (* Apply rotation: tangles = rotvel * FrameTime *)
    let tp =
      Ox_math.fixmul
        ~a:
          (let x, _, _ = rotvel in
           x)
        ~b:frame_time
    in
    let th =
      Ox_math.fixmul
        ~a:
          (let _, y, _ = rotvel in
           y)
        ~b:frame_time
    in
    let tb =
      Ox_math.fixmul
        ~a:
          (let _, _, z = rotvel in
           z)
        ~b:frame_time
    in
    let rotmat = Ox_math.vm_angles_2_matrix ~v:(tp, tb, th) in
    let orient = Ox_math.vm_matrix_x_matrix ~a:orient ~b:rotmat in
    (* Apply turnroll update *)
    let turnroll =
      if flags land pf_turnroll <> 0
      then
        set_object_turnroll

content.thinking:
Wait, let me look at something else. The physics sim orientation in OCaml is only computed once at the start (`do_physics_sim_rot`). Then the movement loop modifies position. But what if there's a D2-specific issue?

Let me look at the D2 physics sim function to see how it differs from D1.

Actually, wait. Let me look at the actual movement loop in the D2 physics sim. The key question is: after `do_physics_sim_rot` computes the orientation, and the movement loop does its thing with FVI, what happens to the orientation when a wall is hit? In C, the wall collision might rotate the object. 

In OCaml, the orientation stays as computed by `do_physics_sim_rot`. But `collide_robot_and_wall` doesn't modify orientation either, so that's not it. Maybe the real issue is in `do_ai_frame` — if it's computing different thrust or rotation values for object 92, that would feed different inputs into the physics sim and cause the divergence. Since OCaml is authoritative for AI, any bug there would show up as position and orientation differences.

Now I'm looking at the divergence summary to find the biggest source. AI local divergences are actually the largest category at 384,725 compared to 247,790 object divergences. The time_since_processed fields are diverging across multiple AI locals, but those are probably cascading from earlier issues. What I really need to do is trace back to the earliest divergence — frame 43, object 92's position bytes — and figure out what object 92 is doing at that point in the game.

Since both the OCaml do_ai_frame and do_physics_sim are authoritative in their respective builds, the position divergence could stem from either a difference in how thrust is calculated during AI frame execution, or how that thrust is applied during physics simulation. There's also the possibility that some sub-function used as an effect is returning different results between builds. Looking at the effects called during physics simulation, I need to check whether Find_vector_intersection, the collision functions, and the various segment lookup functions are all producing consistent results across both implementations.

Wait—Find_vector_intersection is already OCaml-authoritative, which means the OX build uses OCaml's version while the C-ref build uses C's version. If those implementations diverge even slightly, the physics sim would move objects differently, which would explain the position divergence I'm seeing. The question is whether this FVI divergence is new or if it's been stable all along. Given that the divergence rate is 9571 with no regression detected, it seems like this has been a consistent issue rather than something that just appeared. But the actual task is to keep moving forward by porting the next unported C function to OCaml.

Looking at what's still causing divergences, the AI frame effects are the biggest problem at 384725 divergences, which is much larger than the physics sim effects. I need to identify which AI functions are still being called as C effects and prioritize porting the ones that would have the most impact on reducing those divergences.

content.thinking:
There are a LOT of effects in the AI frame. Let me think about this strategically. The earliest divergence is frame 43, object 92, position. Let me think about what commonly happens to robots early in the game:

1. Robots are stationary initially
2. AI frame processes: compute_vis_and_vec, determine behavior
3. If the AI decides to move, it sets thrust
4. Physics sim moves the robot based on thrust

The fact that position/orientation diverge at frame 43 for object 92 means something different is happening to this robot in the OX build vs C-ref build around that frame.

Key insight: if the AI frame (OCaml-authoritative in OX, C in C-ref) makes slightly different decisions about path following or visibility, the robot would get different thrust, leading to different position.
 
If do_ai_frame is supposed to be ported identically but there's still a divergence, it's either a bug in the OCaml implementation, an effect returning different results, or cascading from an earlier divergence. The compute_vis_and_vec effect is critical here—it determines player visibility and drives almost all AI decisions, so if it returns different results between builds, everything downstream cascades. Since compute_vis_and_vec uses FVI which is OCaml-authoritative, the OX build should be using the OCaml version.

The issue is that in the C-ref build, the C FVI is used instead, and these could differ slightly. Rather than chasing down bugs in the already-ported OCaml FVI, I should look at which remaining C effects would give the biggest impact if ported—things like the path creation functions, path following, movement modifiers, and boss AI logic all mutate critical AI state.

Looking at the frequency and impact, `Ai_follow_path` stands out as heavily called and directly affecting thrust and movement, so I should verify whether it's currently in C or if `Compute_vis_and_vec` is actually calling C code through its effect handler.

content.text: Let me check what the most impactful unported effects are. Let me look at `Compute_vis_and_vec` and `Ai_follow_path` - these are called frequently and affect movement.

content.text:
Perfect! Now let me create a summary document with all the key information:

## Analysis Summary

### **FUNCTION 1: `ai_follow_path`**

#### High Level (What it does)
This function controls a robot's pathfinding behavior. It:
1. Maintains a path through segments that the robot follows
2. Handles path creation and regeneration when needed
3. Advances the robot along the waypoints of its path
4. Detects when the robot reaches near a waypoint and picks the next goal
5. Handles special modes like running from the player, escorting, following, patrolling
6. Sets the robot's velocity and orientation toward the next goal point

#### State READ

**object fields:**
- `objp->ctype.ai_info` (ai_static): `hide_index`, `path_length`, `cur_path_index`, `PATH_DIR`, `behavior` (AIB_SNIPE, AIB_STATION, AIB_FOLLOW, etc.), `SUBMODE`
- `objp->pos` (position), `objp->segnum` (segment), `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity`
- `objp->id` (for Robot_info lookup)

**ai_local fields:**
- `ailp->mode` (AIM_RUN_FROM_OBJECT, AIM_STILL, AIM_HIDE, AIM_FOLLOW_PATH, AIM_WANDER, AIM_SNIPE_FIRE, AIM_THIEF_ATTACK)
- `ailp->player_awareness_type`, `ailp->player_awareness_time`

**Global arrays/data:**
- `Point_segs[]` - path segment waypoints (read throughout)
- `Robot_info[objp->id]` - robot properties (max_speed, thief, companion, attack_type)
- `ConsoleObject`, `Viewer`, `Player_is_dead` - player state
- `Ai_local_info[]` - local AI state per object
- `FrameCount`, `FrameTime`, `Difficulty_level`
- `Game_mode` - for multiplayer checks

#### State MODIFIED

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` - robot velocity modified
- `objp->orient` (orientation matrix) - set by ai_path_set_orient_and_vel

**ai_local fields:**
- `ailp->mode` - changed based on path behavior (AIM_STILL, AIM_FOLLOW_PATH, etc.)
- `ailp->player_awareness_type`, `ailp->player_awareness_time` - updated when player visible

**ai_static fields:**
- `aip->cur_path_index` - advances along path
- `aip->PATH_DIR` - may reverse direction
- `aip->path_length` - recalculated by create_n_segment_path/etc

#### Sub-functions Called
- `create_path()` / `create_n_segment_path()` - create or regenerate path
- `move_object_to_goal()` - teleport object to a waypoint (far-away optimization)
- `ai_path_garbage_collect()` - cleanup
- `ai_reset_all_paths()` - reset all paths
- `polish_path()` - optimize path (D2 only, for buddy)
- `create_path_to_player()` - new path toward player
- `create_path_to_station()` - new path back to station
- `find_vector_intersection()` - check if opposite end is reachable (non-snipe mode, end-of-path logic)
- `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel()` - set velocity and orientation toward next goal

#### Complexity
- **D1**: Lines 735-983 (~250 lines)
- **D2**: Lines 1019-1324 (~305 lines)
- **Branches**: Very high. Complex nested if/else, while loop with multiple path-end handling cases
- **Key logic**: Main loop that advances `cur_path_index`, handles edge cases (reach end, hit walls, switch modes)

#### D1 vs D2 Differences
1. **Function signature**: D2 takes 4 args (player_visibility, previous_visibility, vec_to_player), D1 takes 1 arg (player_visibility only)
2. **SNIPE behavior**: D2 handles AIB_SNIPE with thief check; D1 doesn't have snipe
3. **Buddy bot**: D2 has Escort_special_goal check for buddy behavior
4. **path_end logic**: D2 has additional AIB_SNIPE and AIB_FOLLOW checks
5. **Final call**: D1 calls `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel(objp, &goal_point)`, D2 calls it with extra args: `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel(objp, &goal_point, player_visibility, vec_to_player)`
6. **D1 has early return**: D1 checks `(aip->SUBMODE == AISM_HIDING) && (aip->behavior == AIB_HIDE)` and returns early
7. **D1 uses `create_path()` vs D2 uses `create_n_segment_path(5, -1)`** for initial path creation

---

### **FUNCTION 2: `move_away_from_player`**

#### High Level (What it does)
Applies forces to move a robot away from the player vector. This is a **simple velocity modifier**:
1. Subtracts player direction from robot velocity (push away)
2. If attack_type is set, adds evasion velocity (perpendicular directions)
3. Caps final velocity to max_speed

#### State READ

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` (in)
- `objp->orient.uvec`, `objp->orient.rvec` (unit and right vectors for evasion)
- `objp->id` (for Robot_info lookup)

**Parameters passed in:**
- `vec_to_player` (vector pointing toward player)
- `attack_type` (determines if evasion movement applied)

**Global/computed values:**
- `Robot_info[objp->id].max_speed[Difficulty_level]`
- `FrameTime` (frame delta)
- `FrameCount` (for seeding evasion direction)

#### State MODIFIED

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` - modified (written back in both D1 and D2)

Nothing else modified.

#### Sub-functions Called

In C fallback path (non-bridge):
- `vm_vec_scale_add2()` - vector ops (add scaled vector in-place)
- `vm_vec_mag_quick()` - magnitude calculation
- `fixmul()` - fixed-point multiply

All pure math; no AI or path functions.

#### Complexity
- **Both D1 and D2**: ~50 lines of actual code (ignoring #ifdef guards)
- **Branches**: Low. Simple conditional for attack_type, one switch statement
- **Structure**: Straightforward math operations, no loops

#### D1 vs D2 Differences
**These functions are almost identical.** The C fallback code is nearly word-for-word the same:
- Same velocity modification logic
- Same evasion calculation
- Same speed capping
- **No functional difference** between D1 and D2 (both compiled with USE_OX_BRIDGE already)

Both already have OCaml bridge callouts (`cd_ox_move_away_from_player`) but the C fallback is identical.

---

## Recommendation: Which to Port First?

### **Port `move_away_from_player` first**

**Reasons:**
1. **Simplest logic**: Pure vector math, no branches, no sub-functions to port
2. **Smallest code**: ~40 lines of actual C code
3. **Lowest dependencies**: Only calls `vm_vec_scale_add2()`, `vm_vec_mag_quick()`, `fixmul()` (all already ported to OCaml math libs)
4. **Easiest validation**: Deterministic output (except for pseudo-random evasion direction, which is seeded)
5. **D1/D2 identical**: Write once, use for both
6. **Bridge already exists**: The OCaml callout is already there; just need to fill in the OCaml implementation

### **`ai_follow_path` is much harder**

**Reasons:**
1. **Large + complex**: 250-300 lines with deep nesting and multiple branches
2. **Many dependencies**: Calls 7+ sub-functions, many of which aren't ported yet (create_path, create_n_segment_path, find_vector_intersection)
3. **Global state mutations**: Modifies ai_static, ai_local, and calls functions that mutate global Point_segs array
4. **Different D1/D2 signatures**: Different parameters and logic paths, harder to unify
5. **Hard to test**: Behavior is mode-dependent and path-dependent; divergences will be complex

**Porting `move_away_from_player` first gives you:**
- A quick win (simple, testable, working)
- Practice with the bridge architecture
- Evidence that small functions are easy to port
- A foundation for more complex functions later

6. Sets the robot's velocity and orientation toward the next goal point

#### State READ

**object fields:**
- `objp->ctype.ai_info` (ai_static): `hide_index`, `path_length`, `cur_path_index`, `PATH_DIR`, `behavior` (AIB_SNIPE, AIB_STATION, AIB_FOLLOW, etc.), `SUBMODE`
- `objp->pos` (position), `objp->segnum` (segment), `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity`
- `objp->id` (for Robot_info lookup)

**ai_local fields:**
- `ailp->mode` (AIM_RUN_FROM_OBJECT, AIM_STILL, AIM_HIDE, AIM_FOLLOW_PATH, AIM_WANDER, AIM_SNIPE_FIRE, AIM_THIEF_ATTACK)
- `ailp->player_awareness_type`, `ailp->player_awareness_time`

**Global arrays/data:**
- `Point_segs[]` - path segment waypoints (read throughout)
- `Robot_info[objp->id]` - robot properties (max_speed, thief, companion, attack_type)
- `ConsoleObject`, `Viewer`, `Player_is_dead` - player state
- `Ai_local_info[]` - local AI state per object
- `FrameCount`, `FrameTime`, `Difficulty_level`
- `Game_mode` - for multiplayer checks

#### State MODIFIED

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` - robot velocity modified
- `objp->orient` (orientation matrix) - set by ai_path_set_orient_and_vel

**ai_local fields:**
- `ailp->mode` - changed based on path behavior (AIM_STILL, AIM_FOLLOW_PATH, etc.)
- `ailp->player_awareness_type`, `ailp->player_awareness_time` - updated when player visible

**ai_static fields:**
- `aip->cur_path_index` - advances along path
- `aip->PATH_DIR` - may reverse direction
- `aip->path_length` - recalculated by create_n_segment_path/etc

#### Sub-functions Called
- `create_path()` / `create_n_segment_path()` - create or regenerate path
- `move_object_to_goal()` - teleport object to a waypoint (far-away optimization)
- `ai_path_garbage_collect()` - cleanup
- `ai_reset_all_paths()` - reset all paths
- `polish_path()` - optimize path (D2 only, for buddy)
- `create_path_to_player()` - new path toward player
- `create_path_to_station()` - new path back to station
- `find_vector_intersection()` - check if opposite end is reachable (non-snipe mode, end-of-path logic)
- `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel()` - set velocity and orientation toward next goal

#### Complexity
- **D1**: Lines 735-983 (~250 lines)
- **D2**: Lines 1019-1324 (~305 lines)
- **Branches**: Very high. Complex nested if/else, while loop with multiple path-end handling cases
- **Key logic**: Main loop that advances `cur_path_index`, handles edge cases (reach end, hit walls, switch modes)

#### D1 vs D2 Differences
1. **Function signature**: D2 takes 4 args (player_visibility, previous_visibility, vec_to_player), D1 takes 1 arg (player_visibility only)
2. **SNIPE behavior**: D2 handles AIB_SNIPE with thief check; D1 doesn't have snipe
3. **Buddy bot**: D2 has Escort_special_goal check for buddy behavior
4. **path_end logic**: D2 has additional AIB_SNIPE and AIB_FOLLOW checks
5. **Final call**: D1 calls `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel(objp, &goal_point)`, D2 calls it with extra args: `ai_path_set_orient_and_vel(objp, &goal_point, player_visibility, vec_to_player)`
6. **D1 has early return**: D1 checks `(aip->SUBMODE == AISM_HIDING) && (aip->behavior == AIB_HIDE)` and returns early
7. **D1 uses `create_path()` vs D2 uses `create_n_segment_path(5, -1)`** for initial path creation

---

### **FUNCTION 2: `move_away_from_player`**

#### High Level (What it does)
Applies forces to move a robot away from the player vector. This is a **simple velocity modifier**:
1. Subtracts player direction from robot velocity (push away)
2. If attack_type is set, adds evasion velocity (perpendicular directions)
3. Caps final velocity to max_speed

#### State READ

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` (in)
- `objp->orient.uvec`, `objp->orient.rvec` (unit and right vectors for evasion)
- `objp->id` (for Robot_info lookup)

**Parameters passed in:**
- `vec_to_player` (vector pointing toward player)
- `attack_type` (determines if evasion movement applied)

**Global/computed values:**
- `Robot_info[objp->id].max_speed[Difficulty_level]`
- `FrameTime` (frame delta)
- `FrameCount` (for seeding evasion direction)

#### State MODIFIED

**object fields:**
- `objp->mtype.phys_info.velocity` - modified (written back in both D1 and D2)

Nothing else modified.

#### Sub-functions Called

In C fallback path (non-bridge):
- `vm_vec_scale_add2()` - vector ops (add scaled vector in-place)
- `vm_vec_mag_quick()` - magnitude calculation
- `fixmul()` - fixed-point multiply

All pure math; no AI or path functions.

#### Complexity
- **Both D1 and D2**: ~50 lines of actual code (ignoring #ifdef guards)
- **Branches**: Low. Simple conditional for attack_type, one switch statement
- **Structure**: Straightforward math operations, no loops

#### D1 vs D2 Differences
**These functions are almost identical.** The C fallback code is nearly word-for-word the same:
- Same velocity modification logic
- Same evasion calculation
- Same speed capping
- **No functional difference** between D1 and D2 (both compiled with USE_OX_BRIDGE already)

Both already have OCaml bridge callouts (`cd_ox_move_away_from_player`) but the C fallback is identical.

---

## Recommendation: Which to Port First?

### **Port `move_away_from_player` first**

**Reasons:**
1. **Simplest logic**: Pure vector math, no branches, no sub-functions to port
2. **Smallest code**: ~40 lines of actual C code
3. **Lowest dependencies**: Only calls `vm_vec_scale_add2()`, `vm_vec_mag_quick()`, `fixmul()` (all already ported to OCaml math libs)
4. **Easiest validation**: Deterministic output (except for pseudo-random evasion direction, which is seeded)
5. **D1/D2 identical**: Write once, use for both
6. **Bridge already exists**: The OCaml callout is already there; just need to fill in the OCaml implementation

### **`ai_follow_path` is much harder**

**Reasons:**
1. **Large + complex**: 250-300 lines with deep nesting and multiple branches
2. **Many dependencies**: Calls 7+ sub-functions, many of which aren't ported yet (create_path, create_n_segment_path, find_vector_intersection)
3. **Global state mutations**: Modifies ai_static, ai_local, and calls functions that mutate global Point_segs array
4. **Different D1/D2 signatures**: Different parameters and logic paths, harder to unify
5. **Hard to test**: Behavior is mode-dependent and path-dependent; divergences will be complex

**Porting `move_away_from_player` first gives you:**
- A quick win (simple, testable, working)
- Practice with the bridge architecture
- Evidence that small functions are easy to port
- A foundation for more complex functions later

Let me compare C vs OCaml more carefully for the wall bounce code:

C code (line 1293-1369):
1. `vm_vec_sub(&moved_v, &obj->pos, &save_pos)` → OCaml: `vm_vec_sub ~a:(cur_pos) ~b:(save_pos)` ✓
2. `wall_part = vm_vec_dot(&moved_v, &hit_info.hit_wallnorm)` → OCaml: `vm_vec_dotprod ~a:mv ~b:wn` ✓
3. `if (wall_part != 0 && moved_time>0 && (hit_speed=-fixdiv(wall_part,moved_time))>0)` → OCaml checks same conditions ✓


4. The rest of the bounce sequence—collision handling, dead object checks, force field detection, stick logic, wall part recalculation, bounce velocity scaling, and final velocity validation—all map correctly between the two implementations ✓

Now I'm noticing a potential issue: in the C code, `collide_object_with_wall` can modify the object's velocity through collision handlers, and then the wall_part gets recalculated using that updated velocity. But in the OCaml version, after the collision effect runs, the velocity variables aren't refreshed—they still hold the pre-collision values when wall_part2 gets computed. But in C, `obj->mtype.phys_info.velocity` at line 1334 IS the post-collision velocity (modified by collide_object_with_wall). So the wall_part2 computation uses DIFFERENT velocity values between OCaml and C!

This could be a significant bug! Let me verify...

In C, the collision handler modifies the object's velocity directly, then wall_part uses that updated velocity. In OCaml, the effect only returns flags while the velocity remains unchanged from before the collision. But wait—this is actually the expected behavior according to the architecture rules. The collide_object_with_wall effect can only return flags, not velocity changes, since OCaml doesn't have access to the C object's modified state. The proper fix here is to port the collision handler to OCaml entirely, not try to sync the velocity back across the bridge.

For robots specifically, the collision handler doesn't modify velocity anyway—it just opens doors. So the pre-collision and post-collision velocities should be identical, meaning wall_part2 would compute the same value in both C and OCaml. This shouldn't cause divergence for object 92.

So this isn't what's causing the frame-43 divergence. I need to look elsewhere for what's actually different between the C and OCaml execution paths for this robot.

Maybe the issue is earlier in the pipeline—either in how the FVI result is computed or in the AI decision logic. Or possibly there's a bug in how the object's state gets packed when C calls into OCaml's physics simulation, which could cause the velocity to be read differently in each version.

So if earlier decisions diverge and cause robots to take different paths, they'll end up with different velocity and orientation values, which cascades into further divergences downstream.

Porting `ai_follow_path` to pure OCaml wouldn't actually help reduce divergences, since both builds would still receive different inputs from the FVI differences. The real solution would be fixing the OCaml FVI implementation to match C more closely, or porting functions that both builds call through the same code path—but that's not how the architecture works right now.

Actually, I'm realizing the issue: when the C effect handler calls `ai_follow_path`, it modifies velocity and orientation directly on the C object, but those changes never get read back into OCaml's stale velocity references. So in the OX build, velocity stays whatever C set it to, while thrust gets overwritten by OCaml's return values. Then when physics_sim runs, it reads that mixed state—C's velocity with OCaml's thrust—creating the divergence.

The real problem is that this cascades: any initial FVI difference causes different AI decisions, which produce different velocities, which lead to different positions, which create different FVI results next frame, and everything snowballs from there.
